title: Spaghetti cacio e pepe
subtitle: Spaghetti with pecorino and a fierce amount of black pepper
excerpt: Cacio e pepe is one of Rome's three great pasta dishes. Spaghetti, pecorino romano, and black pepper: nothing else. The technique is in the emulsification of cheese and pasta water into a sauce that coats the pasta without clumping or becoming gluey.
sourceNotes: Roman pasta dish. Cacio e pepe (cheese and pepper) is one of the classic Roman pasta preparations alongside carbonara and amatriciana. It uses only pasta, aged Pecorino Romano, black pepper, and pasta water. The dish is deceptively difficult due to the emulsification step.

content.text: Cacio e pepe has three ingredients and one technique that decides whether it works. The cheese must not be added to hot pasta over direct heat or it will seize into clumps. Instead, a slurry of grated cheese and pasta water is prepared off the heat, the pasta is added while both are still warm, and the whole thing is tossed vigorously. The starchy pasta water emulsifies the cheese into a sauce. If the pan is too hot, the cheese cooks and the dish fails.

items.prepNote: Pecorino Romano, finely grated on a microplane

content.text: Cook the pasta and toast the pepper
Bring a large pot of salted water to a rolling boil. Cook the spaghetti until 1 minute before the packet says it is done. Reserve at least 300 ml of pasta cooking water before draining.
While the pasta cooks, toast the crushed pepper in a dry large saucepan for about 1 minute over a medium heat until fragrant. Remove from the heat.
Put the grated pecorino in a bowl. Add 100 ml of hot pasta water and mix to a smooth, creamy paste.
Add a ladleful of pasta water to the saucepan with the toasted pepper and swirl to combine. Add the drained pasta and toss over a very low heat. Remove the pan from the heat.
Pour the cheese paste over the pasta and toss vigorously and continuously, adding more pasta water a splash at a time until you have a creamy, glossy sauce that coats every strand. Serve immediately.

items.cause: The pan was too hot when the cheese was added.
items.fix: Always take the pan off the heat before adding the cheese paste. The residual heat in the pasta and pan is enough to melt it.

items.symptom: The sauce is too thick and sticky
items.fix: Add more pasta water a splash at a time, tossing after each addition, until the sauce is loose and creamy.

items.symptom: The sauce is too loose and watery
items.cause: Too much pasta water, or the pasta was not warm enough.
items.fix: Toss over a very gentle heat for 30 seconds to bring it together. The starch in the water thickens as it heats.

items.cause: Pepper was not toasted, or too coarsely ground.
items.fix: Toast the pepper until fragrant. This mellows the raw harshness and adds a slightly nutty depth.

content.text: Cacio e pepe is one of the four classic Roman pasta dishes alongside amatriciana, carbonara, and gricia. It predates the tomato in Italian cooking: pepper and aged sheep's milk cheese were the defining flavours of the Roman pastoral tradition. The dish is now found across Italy and internationally, though disputes about technique are fierce among its practitioners. The home cook's best approach is to treat it as a technique rather than a recipe.
